quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Linearity"?

options.text: If f'(c) = 0 and f''(c) > 0: local minimum (concave up); f''(c) < 0: local maximum (concave down)
The tolerance for how close f(x) must be to L; represents the desired precision of the output
∫ₐᵇ [αf(x) + βg(x)]dx = α∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx + β∫ₐᵇ g(x)dx; integrals distribute over addition and factor out constants
A constant solution y = c where f(x,c) = 0 for all x; appears as horizontal segments in the slope field

quiz_questions.explanation: "Linearity" is defined as: ∫ₐᵇ [αf(x) + βg(x)]dx = α∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx + β∫ₐᵇ g(x)dx; integrals distribute over addition and factor out constants. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Interval additivity"?

options.text: P = K: if the population is perturbed slightly above or below K, it returns to K
F(x) = P(X ≤ x) = ∫₋∞ˣ f(t)dt; F'(x) = f(x) by FTC; F ranges from 0 to 1
Priority for choosing u: Logarithmic > Inverse trig > Algebraic > Trigonometric > Exponential; u is differentiated, dv is integrated
∫ₐᵇ f dx + ∫ᵇᶜ f dx = ∫ₐᶜ f dx; splitting or combining integration intervals

quiz_questions.explanation: "Interval additivity" is defined as: ∫ₐᵇ f dx + ∫ᵇᶜ f dx = ∫ₐᶜ f dx; splitting or combining integration intervals. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Reversing limits"?

options.text: ∫ₐᵇ f dx = -∫ᵇₐ f dx; switching the limits of integration negates the integral
State the theorem precisely, verify hypotheses, apply the conclusion, and interpret in the context of the problem
Average value has the same units as f(x); the (1/(b-a)) factor divides out the length units
μ = E[X] = ∫₋∞^∞ x·f(x)dx; the 'center of mass' of the probability distribution

quiz_questions.explanation: "Reversing limits" is defined as: ∫ₐᵇ f dx = -∫ᵇₐ f dx; switching the limits of integration negates the integral. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Zero-width integral"?

options.text: If f' changes from + to - at c: local maximum; from - to +: local minimum; no sign change: neither
Priority for choosing u: Logarithmic > Inverse trig > Algebraic > Trigonometric > Exponential; u is differentiated, dv is integrated
0/0, ∞/∞, 0·∞, ∞-∞, 0⁰, 1^∞, ∞⁰; L'Hôpital's directly handles the first two
∫ₐᵃ f dx = 0; integrating over an interval of width zero gives zero

quiz_questions.explanation: "Zero-width integral" is defined as: ∫ₐᵃ f dx = 0; integrating over an interval of width zero gives zero. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Comparison property"?

options.text: A point where the concavity of f changes; requires f''(x) = 0 or undefined AND a sign change in f''
Two functions are asymptotically equivalent if lim(x→∞) f(x)/g(x) = 1
If f' changes from + to - at c: local maximum; from - to +: local minimum; no sign change: neither
If f(x) ≥ g(x) on [a,b], then ∫ₐᵇ f dx ≥ ∫ₐᵇ g dx

quiz_questions.explanation: "Comparison property" is defined as: If f(x) ≥ g(x) on [a,b], then ∫ₐᵇ f dx ≥ ∫ₐᵇ g dx. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "∫ₐᵇ [αf(x) + βg(x)]dx = α∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx + β∫ₐᵇ g(x)dx; integrals distribute over addition and factor out constants"?

options.text: Second derivative test

d/dx[f(x)^g(x)]
Limit laws

options.text: Left/Right/Midpoint sum
Verification technique

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "If f(x) ≥ g(x) on [a,b], then ∫ₐᵇ f dx ≥ ∫ₐᵇ g dx"?

Rectilinear motion

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Definite Integral Properties is accurate?

options.text: If f'(x) > 0 on an interval, then f is increasing on that interval; if f'(x) < 0, f is decreasing.
Definite Integral Properties provide algebraic rules for working with integrals.
Arc length of y = f(x) from a to b: L = ∫ₐᵇ √(1 + [f'(x)]²)dx.
The fundamental pair: d/dx[sin x] = cos x and d/dx[cos x] = -sin x (derived from the limit definition using lim(h→0) sin(h)/h = 1).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Definite Integral Properties as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Definite Integral Properties?

options.text: Antiderivatives: Integration Strategies develops a systematic approach to choosing the right technique.
The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus connects differentiation and integration: d/dx[∫ₐˣ f(t)dt] = f(x).
The chain rule 'peels off layers' of composition: differentiate the outer function (leaving inner unchanged), then multiply by the derivative of the inner.

Key properties: ∫ₐᵇ [f ± g]dx = ∫ₐᵇ f dx ± ∫ₐᵇ g dx (linearity); ∫ₐᵇ cf dx = c∫ₐᵇ f dx (constant multiple); ∫ₐᵇ f dx + ∫ᵇᶜ f dx = ∫ₐᶜ f dx (additivity over intervals); ∫ₐᵃ f dx = 0; ∫ₐᵇ f dx = -∫ᵇₐ f dx (reversing limits).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Definite Integral Properties as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Definite Integral Properties?

options.text: The theorem fails if the interval is open (extrema may not be attained), the function is not continuous (could have gaps), or the domain is unbounded.
It is the theoretical foundation for many calculus results.
If f(x) ≥ 0 on [a,b], then ∫ₐᵇ f dx ≥ 0 (non-negativity).
Success requires clear justification of setup, correct antiderivatives, appropriate use of technology, and precise interpretation of results in the given context.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Definite Integral Properties as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Definite Integral Properties?

options.text: The fundamental pair: d/dx[sin x] = cos x and d/dx[cos x] = -sin x (derived from the limit definition using lim(h→0) sin(h)/h = 1).
Combined with the chain rule: d/dx[e^(g(x))] = e^(g(x)) · g'(x) and d/dx[ln(g(x))] = g'(x)/g(x).
Definite Integral Properties provide algebraic rules for working with integrals.
Drug dosing models use DEs to track drug concentration in the bloodstream.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Definite Integral Properties as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Definite Integral Properties?

options.text: Key properties: ∫ₐᵇ [f ± g]dx = ∫ₐᵇ f dx ± ∫ₐᵇ g dx (linearity); ∫ₐᵇ cf dx = c∫ₐᵇ f dx (constant multiple); ∫ₐᵇ f dx + ∫ᵇᶜ f dx = ∫ₐᶜ f dx (additivity over intervals); ∫ₐᵃ f dx = 0; ∫ₐᵇ f dx = -∫ᵇₐ f dx (reversing limits).
Solutions are functions y(x) that satisfy the equation.
These diverse applications demonstrate that integration is a universal tool for accumulating continuously varying quantities.
Second Derivative Test provides a quick classification of critical points using concavity.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Definite Integral Properties as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Definite Integral Properties?

options.text: Minimizing travel time: finding the optimal route combining different speed zones (e.g., running on sand vs. swimming) using calculus of optimization
Decomposing complex problems: splitting ∫₀¹⁰ f dx into ∫₀⁵ f dx + ∫₅¹⁰ f dx when the function has different behavior on each interval
Computing moments of inertia in physics: ∫x²·f(x)dx often requires integration by parts, essential for structural and mechanical engineering
Cooling/heating: Newton's law of cooling dT/dt = -k(T-Tₛ) is separable, giving T(t) = Tₛ + (T₀-Tₛ)e^(-kt)

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Definite Integral Properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Definite Integral Properties?

options.text: Physics: work done by a varying force over a complex path can be split into segments using interval additivity
Medical imaging: CT scans reconstruct 3D images by computing rates of change of X-ray attenuation through tissue from multiple angles
Financial markets: the 'Greeks' (delta, gamma, theta) in options pricing are derivatives that measure how option value changes with respect to stock price, rate of change, and time
Surveillance camera tracking: as a car drives past a fixed camera, the rate of angle change dθ/dt determines how fast the camera must pan to keep the car in frame

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Definite Integral Properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Definite Integral Properties applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Climate data: identifying periods of warming (increasing temperature trend) vs. cooling using derivative analysis of temperature time series
Bacterial growth: E. coli doubles every 20 minutes in ideal conditions; 1 bacterium becomes ~70 billion in 12 hours (2³⁶ ≈ 6.9 × 10¹⁰)
Estimating total rainfall from rate-of-rainfall data: ∫(rainfall rate)dt gives total accumulated precipitation over a storm event
Financial modeling: total revenue over a year can be split into quarterly integrals for seasonal analysis

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Definite Integral Properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Definite Integral Properties?

options.text: Total rainfall accumulation: if r(t) is the rainfall rate at time t, then total accumulated rainfall by time x is R(x) = ∫₀ˣ r(t)dt
Decomposing complex problems: splitting ∫₀¹⁰ f dx into ∫₀⁵ f dx + ∫₅¹⁰ f dx when the function has different behavior on each interval
Rocket propulsion: as fuel is burned, the rocket becomes lighter, changing the force equation—integration handles the continuously varying mass
Numerical integration in computer science: trapezoidal and Simpson's rules are programmed into calculators and software for approximating integrals when antiderivatives don't exist in closed form

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Definite Integral Properties.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Definite Integral Properties in practice?

options.text: Minimizing travel time: finding the optimal route combining different speed zones (e.g., running on sand vs. swimming) using calculus of optimization
Economics: ∫₀ᵀ (revenue rate - cost rate)dt = total profit over the period [0,T]
Medical imaging: CT scans reconstruct 3D images by computing rates of change of X-ray attenuation through tissue from multiple angles
Physics: work done by a varying force over a complex path can be split into segments using interval additivity

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Definite Integral Properties.
